title: Osteoporosis Prevention and Bone Health in the Older Adult
subtitle: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman with a DEXA showing osteopenia attends a wellness visit. The nurse teaches bone-protective habits — calcium and vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, fall-proofing, and safe bisphosphonate use — to prevent fragility fractures.

patient.name: G. Bennett
patient.admitDate: Outpatient wellness visit. Postmenopausal (menopause at 51), maternal history of hip fracture, sedentary, low dairy intake, former smoker. DEXA shows osteopenia. Here for bone-health teaching.

labs.r: Act before more bone is lost.

labs.v: Remove rugs; add lighting + grab bars.

labs.r: Empty stomach, full glass of water.

nursesNote: G. Bennett, 62, is postmenopausal with several osteoporosis risk factors (menopause at 51, maternal hip fracture, sedentary lifestyle, low dairy intake, former smoker). Her DEXA shows osteopenia (T-score -2.0). At this wellness visit the nurse teaches bone-protective habits: aim for about 1200 mg of calcium per day from food first plus roughly 800-1000 IU of vitamin D; perform regular weight-bearing and resistance exercise; and fall-proof the home (remove loose rugs, improve lighting, add grab bars) since most fragility fractures follow a fall. The nurse reviews smoking and alcohol moderation, the role of DEXA screening and T-score interpretation, and, if a bisphosphonate is prescribed, safe administration: take it in the morning on an empty stomach with a full glass of plain water and remain upright for at least 30 minutes. Teaching is confirmed with teach-back before the visit ends.
providerOrders: Counsel ~1200 mg/day calcium (food first) and ~800-1000 IU/day vitamin D
Encourage regular weight-bearing and resistance exercise
Review home fall-proofing to prevent fragility fractures
Reinforce smoking cessation and alcohol moderation; offer support
If a bisphosphonate is started, teach upright-30-minutes, empty-stomach, full-glass-of-water administration

opts.text: Postmenopausal status, a family history of hip fracture, and inactivity raise osteoporosis risk
Adequate calcium and vitamin D support bone health
Weight-bearing and resistance exercise help build and maintain bone
Most fragility fractures in older adults follow a fall, so fall-proofing matters
Bed rest and avoiding activity are the best way to protect fragile bones
Smoking and heavy alcohol use weaken bone and should be addressed
Calcium is best obtained from food first, with supplements to fill gaps
A DEXA T-score of -1 to -2.5 indicates osteopenia; -2.5 or lower indicates osteoporosis
A bisphosphonate is taken on an empty stomach with water, staying upright for 30 minutes
DEXA screening is recommended for women 65 and older, or earlier with added risk
Lying down right after a bisphosphonate dose is recommended
Vitamin D helps the body absorb calcium
Review DEXA results and personal risk factors
Optimize calcium and vitamin D intake
Add weight-bearing and resistance exercise
Fall-proof the home environment
Reinforce smoking/alcohol moderation and schedule follow-up
Aim for about 1200 mg of calcium per day, food first
Take about 800-1000 IU of vitamin D daily
Do regular weight-bearing and resistance exercise
Remove loose rugs and add grab bars and lighting at home
If on a bisphosphonate, stay upright 30 minutes after the dose
Stay sedentary to protect fragile bones
Lie down right after taking a bisphosphonate
Continue heavy alcohol use, assuming it won't affect bone

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess current diet, activity, and home-safety habits
Teach calcium and vitamin D targets and food sources
Review safe weight-bearing and resistance exercise
Cover home fall-proofing and safe bisphosphonate use if prescribed
Use teach-back to confirm understanding and set follow-up
"I'll get calcium from food first and take vitamin D every day."
"I'll do regular walking and some light resistance exercise."
"I'll remove throw rugs and add night lights and grab bars."
"I'll take my bone pill with a full glass of water and stay upright 30 minutes."
"I should rest in bed most of the day so I don't break a bone."
"I can lie down right after taking my bone pill."
"Smoking and heavy drinking won't affect my bones."
"I don't need a bone-density scan even with my risk factors."
